summary: Gruber responds to criticism that Tweetbot feels 'like an Android app' by arguing that people making this comparison haven't actually used Android extensively. He contends that Tweetbot's heavily art-directed Tapbots aesthetic feels foreign to iOS but bears no actual resemblance to Android's UI. Through comparing Tweetbot to real Android Twitter clients like Plume, he illustrates the vast design gap between the platforms. He concludes that iOS aims for 'magic' in its design philosophy while Android deliberately eschews it in favor of pure functionality.
key_points: Critics calling Tweetbot 'Android-like' are conflating 'foreign to iOS' with 'actually resembling Android,' which it doesn't
The Tapbots aesthetic is more art-directed than UI-designed, making apps feel like games rather than tools — which is inherently divisive
Android's best-designed apps like Plume wouldn't garner attention on iOS, illustrating a 'gaping chasm' in aesthetic design between the platforms
There are legitimate users who prefer Android's purely functional approach and would hold up Tweetbot as everything wrong with iOS
The fundamental design difference between iOS and Android can be captured in one word: magic — iOS aims for it, Android doesn't want it
money_quotes: I'm pretty sure none of the people saying this have used an Android device extensively.
The Tapbots aesthetic isn't so much UI-designed as art-directed.
Using Tweetbot is like playing a game of Twitter.
The real Android has a system-wide UI aesthetic with little personality, and no soul.
That this is so exemplifies the gaping chasm between iOS and Android in terms of aesthetic design.
The difference between iOS and Android, design-wise, can be summed in a single word: magic. iOS aims for it. Android doesn't want it.
Not in the artistic, warm, humanist way that the Tapbots aesthetic is bot-like, but in the cool, rational, science-not-art, thinking-as-opposed-to-feeling sense of artificial intelligence.
key_insight: Calling Tweetbot 'Android-like' reveals more about iOS users' imagined idea of Android than about either platform's actual design philosophy — the real distinction is that iOS pursues magic while Android deliberately opts for pure rationality.
